content:
TypeScript overtook Python and JavaScript as the most-used language on GitHub this year. I write it every day, and it still didn’t stop me from shipping a form that quietly broke on doctypes it had never seen.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There’s a specific kind of confidence TypeScript gives you. Types compile, the editor stops complaining, and something in your brain files the code as “safe.” I felt that confidence the day I built mapOptions, a small utility meant to convert Frappe doctype option lists into the label-value pairs Ant Design's select components expect. Clean interface, fully typed, worked perfectly against the three doctypes I tested it on.
It broke on the fourth. Look at the code below before I explain what went wrong. See if you can spot it yourself; it took me embarrassingly long.
interface DocFieldOption {
label: string;
value: string;
}function mapOptions(optionsString: string): DocFieldOption[] {
return optionsString
.split('\n')
.map(opt => ({ label: opt, value: opt }));
}
The type system was telling the truth about the wrong thing
Frappe doctypes are schema definitions that live in the database, editable through the backend desk UI, often by people who aren’t frontend developers and have no reason to think about what a React component expects from them. A doctype’s option field is usually a newline-separated string of values. Most of the time. Not always.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The fourth doctype had a field where someone had added a stray blank line between two options while editing it through Frappe’s admin interface, something like "Wheat\nRice\n\nMaize". My mapOptions function had a TypeScript interface describing exactly what I expected each entry to look like, and TypeScript happily confirmed my function honored its own contract: every element it returned matched DocFieldOption, { label: string, value: string }, No complaints. What TypeScript couldn't tell me, because it has no visibility into it, was whether the actual runtime data would ever contain an empty string masquerading as a valid label. It did. .split('\n') turned that blank line into '', which my map function dutifully wrapped into { label: '', value: '' }, rendered as a blank, un-selectable row sitting silently inside a production dropdown.
TypeScript wasn’t wrong. It was answering a question I hadn’t actually asked it. I’d asked “does this function handle the shape I described?” and it said yes, correctly. I needed an answer to “does the backend’s actual data always match the shape I described,” and that’s a question no amount of interface definitions can answer, because the backend doesn’t know TypeScript exists.
Where the confidence gap actually lives
This is the part that took me longer to internalize than I’d like to admit: type safety in a React and TypeScript frontend only covers the code you control. The moment data crosses from a Python-based Frappe backend, shaped by admins editing doctype JSON through a UI with no compile step of its own, into your typed frontend, you’re not extending your type guarantees across that boundary. You’re hoping.
A few places this shows up on our stack specifically:

• Doctype field definitions can change without a build failing. Someone updates a select field’s options in production through the desk UI, and nothing in CI catches that your frontend’s assumptions about that field just went stale, because there’s no shared schema enforcing it on both sides.

• Optional versus required is a backend concept that doesn’t automatically propagate. A field marked optional in a doctype JSON doesn’t tell your TypeScript interface anything unless you manually keep them in sync, and drift happens quietly, one doctype edit at a time, usually noticed only when a form breaks.

• any sneaks in at exactly the boundary where it matters most. API response typing on Frappe calls is one of the easiest places to write Promise<any> and move on, because properly typing a dynamic, schema-driven backend response is genuinely tedious. It's also exactly where a bad assumption does the most damage, since that's the seam between two systems that don't share a type system at all.
What I do differently now
I didn’t stop trusting TypeScript. I stopped treating a green compile as evidence about anything outside the frontend. Here’s the fixed version, with a runtime guard instead of a blind trust in the shape:
function mapOptions(optionsString: string): DocFieldOption[] {
return optionsString
.split('\n')
.map(opt => opt.trim())
.filter(Boolean)
.map(opt => ({ label: opt, value: opt }));
}filter(Boolean) drops the blank line before it ever becomes a phantom option. Three added characters worth of logic, and it would have caught the bug on day one instead of on a support ticket three weeks later. For anything higher-stakes than a dropdown, like the payload from a whitelisted API call, I go a step further and validate the actual shape at runtime:
function isValidOptionsResponse(data: unknown): data is DocFieldOption[] {
return Array.isArray(data) &&
data.every(item =>
typeof item === 'object' && item !== null &&
typeof (item as any).label === 'string' && (item as any).label.length > 0 &&
typeof (item as any).value === 'string'
);
}It’s a few extra lines that feel redundant right up until a doctype gets edited in a way nobody told the frontend about, and then it’s the difference between a caught error and a blank row in a production dropdown.
Here’s something worth trying on your own codebase this week: grep for Promise<any> anywhere it touches a call to your backend, and count how many of those calls feed directly into something rendered without a runtime check. I did this after the mapOptions bug. The number surprised me, and not in a good way.
I’ve also started treating doctype JSON diffs as changes worth reviewing with the same seriousness as a frontend pull request, not a backend concern that’s someone else’s problem. If a field’s options, required status, or type changes on a doctype my components consume, that’s a frontend-relevant change even though it never touches a .tsx file. Nobody enforces that connection automatically. I have to make it myself, every time.
The language trend is real, the safety story is incomplete
TypeScript’s rise makes sense. Static types catch a real, large category of bugs, and I’d never go back to plain JavaScript on a project this size. But writing TypeScript on a schema-driven backend that has no idea your type system exists taught me that type safety is a claim about your code, not about your data. The compiler can only promise you handled the shape you described. It has no opinion on whether the world actually sends you that shape, and on a platform where non-developers can reshape your data contract through an admin UI, that gap isn’t theoretical. It’s a Tuesday.
